statement: Microsoft has productised Anthropic-format Agent Skills (SKILL.md) in Azure AI Foundry as a server-side 'versioned Skills API' — every update creates a new immutable version and the parent skill tracks a promotable `default_version` — and delivers skills either by direct download into a hosted/local agent or by attaching them to an MCP toolbox, where they are exposed as MCP Resources that any MCP client (GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, or a custom harness) can discover and read with no Foundry SDK, following the MCP Skills extension (SEP-2640).

evidence.quote: A skill is a `SKILL.md` file that you author once and store centrally in Foundry through the versioned Skills API ... Skills are versioned: every update creates a new immutable version, and the parent skill tracks a `default_version`. ... skills appear as MCP Resources. ... Any MCP client — GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, or your own agent harness — can consume skills this way without any Foundry SDK.

Defines a transport binding for serving Agent Skills over MCP using the Resources primitive, establishing the `skill://` URI scheme. "A skill's URI is directly readable via `resources/read` whether or not it appears in any index."

falsifier: Show Foundry's Skills API does not enforce immutable versions / `default_version` promotion, or that toolbox-attached skills cannot be consumed by a non-Microsoft MCP client without a Foundry SDK — e.g. Microsoft pulls the preview, or SEP-2640 lands in a form generic MCP clients can't read. Verdict would lift to `verified` if an independent (non-Microsoft) source corroborates the versioned-API semantics.
notes: Extends CLM-adv-skill-format-absorbed (Microsoft adopting SKILL.md) with the concrete productisation: a server-side versioned registry + MCP-resource delivery. Verdict held at `plausible` because the versioned-API specifics are single-vendor-sourced (Microsoft only); the MCP-resource delivery path is the part corroborated by the independent SEP-2640. Strategic read for own-agency: the 'central versioned skill registry + promote-to-default' workflow is now a first-party cloud feature, not a gap a third-party build-time resolver uniquely fills.
